summary: Patrick McKenzie and Ramit Sethi argue that underpricing products and services attracts pathological customers who demand more while perceiving less value. Drawing from their own experiences — Ramit going from a $4.95 ebook to $12,000 courses, Patrick 7X-ing his consulting rates — they demonstrate that higher prices correlate with better customers, fewer support headaches, and greater perceived value. They discuss the psychology behind why engineers and freelancers chronically underprice, including fear of rejection and anchoring to hourly wage mental models. The core thesis is that pricing is strategic: it signals what market you play in, what customers you attract, and how seriously your work is taken.
key_points: Cheap customers generate disproportionately more support requests and unreasonable demands — Patrick's $9/month tier generated 7X the support load of his $199/month tier
Engineers underprice because they anchor to hourly wage mental models instead of framing their work as strategic business investments with measurable ROI
Quoting weekly rates instead of hourly rates moves you into a different 'taxonomy of value' where you're compared against strategic initiatives rather than labor costs
Disqualifying bad-fit customers and being willing to turn down money signals trustworthiness and actually increases your perceived value to the right clients
You can price for free or price for dear, but never price for cheap — cheap pricing creates the worst of all worlds with maximum headaches and minimum revenue
money_quotes: You can price it for free or you can price it for dear, but never price it for cheap.

If you are genuinely creating value for businesses, you are no longer in the 'trade defined small units of time for defined small units of money' business model. This is what most employees do. This is how you have spent most of your life working. You have left that life behind.
People have a preconceived notion of the proper cost of one hour of time.
If I know my product is good, if I have tested results, it's my obligation to get it out to the market.
I have never desired to be a peon, so I have always made a point of not talking like a peon.
A lot of fantastic value can be delivered with capable, workmanlike coding.
If you're scheduled at or near capacity, charge more. If losing a client would not meaningfully impact your standard of living, charge more.
We could pay that out of the petty cash.
key_insight: Pricing is not just a revenue decision but a customer-selection mechanism: higher prices attract customers who perceive more value, demand less support, and create better outcomes for everyone involved.
